title: The First Sunday in Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## March 1, 2020 | "The First Sunday in Lent" | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the First Sunday in Lent, the opening Sunday of the six-week penitential season leading to Easter (April 12, 2020). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would be Genesis 2:15-17; 3:1-7 (the Fall of Adam and Eve), Psalm 32, Romans 5:12-19 (sin and grace through Christ), and Matthew 4:1-11 (Jesus's temptation in the wilderness). The sermon falls on the traditional day for blessing ashes and beginning the Lenten fast, though ashes were distributed on Ash Wednesday (February 26) the preceding Wednesday.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a relatively stable period denominationally, with no major General Convention scheduled (triennial meetings occur in odd-numbered years). The Diocese of Colorado was operating under Bishop Kimberly Lucas (elected 2017), who had been navigating routine diocesan governance. No major Episcopal controversies or elections were dominating the news at this specific moment, though the Church was beginning to process implications of pandemic precautions that would escalate dramatically within days.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in early March with mild spring weather and no major weather emergencies immediately preceding this date. The Colorado legislature was in session (commenced January 8, 2020) with the regular session running through mid-May. No specific Capitol Hill neighborhood emergencies or downtown crises dominated local news in the days before March 1. The Cathedral's downtown location would have been experiencing normal urban activity, though awareness of COVID-19 was beginning to emerge in news coverage.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The United States was beginning to grapple with COVID-19's arrival, with the first death reported in Washington State on February 29, 2020 (two days before this sermon). The Democratic primary race was intensifying with Super Tuesday scheduled for March 3, 2020, four days after this sermon. The Trump administration was facing impeachment trial aftermath and was already under criticism for early pandemic response inadequacies. Stock markets had begun showing volatility due to pandemic concerns but had not yet experienced the March crash.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

COVID-19 was dominating global headlines, with Italy entering a severe outbreak (Lombardy lockdown began February 23), South Korea reporting exponential case growth, and Iran struggling with initial cases (outbreak announced February 24). Global markets were showing distress signals in response to pandemic spread. WHO had declared COVID-19 a "Public Health Emergency of International Concern" on January 30, making this March 1 sermon date a moment when the severity of the unfolding pandemic was becoming undeniable.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American society was in a state of emergent dread and denial about COVID-19—many people still viewed it as a distant threat, yet anxiety was visibly rising. The zeitgeist was suspended between normalcy and catastrophe; large gatherings (including religious services) were still happening but with growing unease. This was the last moment many American congregations would gather in-person for months. Katie Pearson's sermon on wilderness temptation and spiritual discipline would have carried an unintentional poignancy, as her congregation was unknowingly standing at the threshold of a national crisis that would transform religious practice for years.
